---
title: "Tau Expression in Response to ProNGF Treatment in BFCNs"
author: ""
date: today
format:
  pptx:
    slide-level: 2
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Background

## Published Context

ProNGF — the precursor form of Nerve Growth Factor — was recently shown to drive
**retrograde axonal degeneration** of basal forebrain cholinergic neurons (BFCNs)
through the p75^NTR^ receptor and induction of amyloid precursor protein (APP).

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
![](figs/SciSignaling_header.png){width=100%}
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
![](figs/SciSignaling_cover.png){width=100%}
:::
::::

---

## The Mechanism

ProNGF acts selectively on axons, triggering a cascade that leads to both
**axon degeneration** and **cell death**:

- Axon-specific proNGF → p75^NTR^ activation
- ↑ Nascent protein synthesis in axons, including **APP**
- APP↑ → Ca²⁺ increase → Calpain activation
- Concurrent retrograde p-JNK/JNK signalling → cell death

![](figs/SciSignaling_mechanism.png){width=55% fig-align="center"}

---

## Prior Lab Data — Ingenuity Pathway Analysis (IPA)

To identify which upstream regulators are activated by axonal proNGF treatment,
BFCNs were cultured in **filter chambers** that physically separate axons from
cell bodies.

- Axons were treated with proNGF for **30 minutes**
- Newly synthesised proteins were labelled with **O-propargyl-puromycin (OPP)**
- After a **2-hour labelling window**, axonal lysates were collected and analysed

IPA of the axonal proteome revealed **MAPT (tau)** and **APP** as the top
upstream regulators (*p* = 4.35×10⁻⁷⁰ and 4.83×10⁻³⁶, respectively),
with predicted downstream effects on cell death and necrosis.

![](figs/SciSignaling_IPA.png){width=75% fig-align="center"}

---

## Tau Isoforms & Protein Structure

**Six CNS isoforms** arise from alternative splicing of exons 2, 3, and 10
of the *MAPT* gene, generating 3R and 4R repeat variants that differ in
microtubule-binding affinity. The protein contains an **N-terminal projection
domain**, a **proline-rich region**, the **microtubule-binding repeat domain
(MTBD)**, and a short C-terminal tail. Phosphorylation sites in this study
(pThr181, AT8, pSer622) all lie outside the MTBD.

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
![Tau isoforms (Alz journal).](figs/Isoforms_of_Tau_alz_journal.jpg){width=100%}
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
![Tau protein structure (ScienceDirect).](figs/Tau_Structure_scidirect.jpg){width=100%}
:::
::::

---

## Tau: Beyond its Canonical Role

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="45%"}
- **Microtubule stabilization and cytoskeletal coordination:**
  Binds axonal microtubules to maintain cytoskeletal integrity and neuronal polarity.
  Integrates microtubule and actin networks to support axonal structure and dynamics.

- **Regulation of axonal transport:**
  Controls kinesin- and dynein-based trafficking of vesicles, organelles, and signaling complexes.

- **Signaling scaffold:**
  Organizes kinase and stress-response pathways linking extracellular signals to intracellular responses.

- **Synaptic function and plasticity:**
  Participates in synaptic signaling, receptor regulation, and activity-dependent local translation of Tau.

- **Proteostasis and clearance:**
  Interacts with chaperones, proteasome, and autophagy machinery to regulate Tau turnover.

- **Nuclear and stress-related roles:**
  Can localize to the nucleus, where it may contribute to DNA protection and stress responses.
:::
::: {.column width="55%"}
![Sinsky, J., Pichlerova, K., & Hanes, J. (2021).](figs/Roles_of_Tau_alz_journal.jpg){width=100%}
:::
::::

---

## Rationale for This Study

The IPA data pointed to **tau (MAPT)** as a central upstream regulator of the
axonal response to proNGF — yet the specific changes in tau protein expression
and phosphorylation state had not been characterised.

**This study asks:**

> Does proNGF treatment alter **total tau** and **phospho-tau** levels
> in the axon and soma compartments of BFCNs?

We used Western blotting with antibodies targeting three phosphorylation sites —
**pThr181**, **pSer202/pThr205 (AT8)**, and **pSer622** — alongside pan-tau,
to capture both the abundance and phosphorylation state of tau across
compartments.

# Total Tau Increase in Response to ProNGF Treatment

## Overview

ProNGF treatment was applied to compartmentalized basal forebrain cholinergic neuron cultures.

Western blotting was used to assess changes in **total Tau** and **phospho-Tau** protein levels across soma and axon compartments.

---

## Tau Phosphorylation Sites for Antibodies Used

In this research, I quantified tau phosphorylation using three antibodies that report distinct tau states:

- **pThr181 (p-tau181)**: commonly used biomarker-associated phosphorylation site that tracks Alzheimer's disease-related tau pathology in CSF and plasma and is widely used for staging/stratification.
  Key kinases: GSK-3β, CDK5.

- **AT8 (pSer202/pThr205)**: canonical neuropathology epitope used to detect early and established pathological tau (pre-tangles/tangles) in tissue; often treated as a marker of disease-associated tau phosphorylation.
  Key kinases: GSK-3β, MAPK, CDK5.

- **pSer622**: a C-terminal tau site assessed in biochemical studies; interpretation depends on antibody specificity and isoform context, and is typically considered alongside broader phosphorylation patterns.
  Key kinases: Casein kinase 1 and 2, GSK-3β.

---

## Phosphorylation Patterns of Tau

![The figure shows the phosphorylation sites of tau identified in each disease and the frequency (Horie et al. and Kametani et al.).](figs/external_pTau_FEB4-14-181-g005.jpg){width=80% fig-align="center"}

---

## Composite Western Blot — All Compartments

![Western blot analysis of Tau and actin expression. Composite image.](figs/ColoredLabeledWB_Feb12.png){width=80% fig-align="center"}

---

## Representative Composite Image

![Representative composite image showing actin (blue) and Tau (green).](figs/ELINA_2026-02-12_12h52m48s_Composite_.jpg){width=80% fig-align="center"}

---

## Green Channel — Actin & Phospho-Tau (800CW)

![Green channel image showing actin (mouse) and phospho-Tau (rabbit; pSer202/pThr205).](figs/ELINA_2026-02-05_12h18m48s_IRDye_800CW_.jpg){width=80% fig-align="center"}

---

## Red Channel — Total Tau (pan-Tau; Rb-237)

![Red channel image showing total Tau (pan-Tau; Rb-237).](figs/ELINA_2026-02-06_13h04m41s_IRDye_680RD_.jpg){width=80% fig-align="center"}

# Soma-Only Repeat Western Blot

## Overview

A repeat Western blot was performed on **soma-only** fractions from the same samples as above.

Targets: **phospho-Tau (pSer622)**, **pan-Tau**, and **actin**.

---

## Soma-Only Composite Western Blot

![Western blot analysis of phosphoTau, panTau and actin expression. Composite image.](figs/ColoredLabeledWB_onlySoma.png){width=80% fig-align="center"}

---

## Red Channel — Phospho-Tau at Ser622 (680RD)

![Red channel image showing phospho-Tau at Ser622 (pSer622).](figs/ELINA_2026-02-11_13h52m00s_IRDye_680RD_.jpg){width=80% fig-align="center"}

---

## Composite — pSer622 & Pan-Tau

![Composite image: phospho-Tau at Ser622 (red channel) and pan-Tau (green channel; Red antibody = Rabbit, Green antibody = Mouse).](figs/ELINA_2026-02-13_10h47m12s_Composite_.jpg){width=80% fig-align="center"}

# Summary

## Key Findings

- IPA of axonal lysates after proNGF treatment identified **MAPT (tau)** and **APP**
  as the top upstream regulators, motivating a direct biochemical assessment of tau
- ProNGF treatment induces changes in **total Tau** levels detectable by Western blot
- Phosphorylation at **pSer202/pThr205** and **pSer622** sites assessed across compartments
- Soma-only repeat blots confirm signal specificity in the **cell body fraction**

**Next steps:** Quantification of band intensities and normalization to actin loading control
